title: Leadership Team Behaviour

introduction.subtitle: Governance assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Leadership Team Behaviour helps you assess Strategic Conflict, Decision Rights, Trust & Accountability, Operating Cadence through concrete operating questions. Answer from observable behaviour and current evidence, not aspiration.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How openly does the leadership team debate strategic trade-offs?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows debating strategic trade-offs; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of debating strategic trade-offs, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show debating strategic trade-offs in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Debating strategic trade-offs is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Strategic Conflict: How openly does the leadership team debate strategic trade-offs
conceptHelp.summary: Strategic Conflict is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How openly does the leadership team debate strategic trade-offs?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Strategic Conflict matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.
